But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
Google Meet Transcript
Session Date: April 3, 2024
Duration: 52:14

[00:00:08]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Hey Marcus, good to see you. How have you been since our last session?

[00:00:13]
Marcus Williams: Hey Doc. I've been... it's been a rough week, honestly.

[00:00:18]:
Dr. James Rodriguez: I'm sorry to hear that. Do you want to tell me what's been going on?

[00:00:23]
Marcus Williams: Yeah. So, remember how we talked last time about the nightmares? They're back. Bad. I had three this week. Same one over and over - the convoy, the explosion, seeing Rodriguez - my buddy Rodriguez, not you - seeing him after the IED went off.

[00:00:45]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That sounds really difficult, Marcus. Have you been using any of the grounding techniques we practiced?

[00:00:52]
Marcus Williams: I try. When I wake up, I'm so jacked up, you know? Heart pounding, sweating through the sheets. Sometimes I can do the 5-4-3-2-1 thing, sometimes I just... I can't. My brain won't slow down. I end up just sitting there in the dark until my wife wakes up and finds me.

[00:01:14]
Dr. James Rodriguez: How is Sarah handling all of this?

[00:01:18]
Marcus Williams: She's a saint, man. I don't know how she puts up with me. But I can see it wearing on her. Last night she asked me if I'd talked to you about the medication thing again. I know she's worried. I'm worried too, but I just... I don't want to be on pills, you know? I saw what happened to guys in my unit who got on that stuff.

[00:01:42]
Dr. James Rodriguez: I understand your concerns about medication. We don't have to make any decisions about that today. Let's talk about what's been triggering the nightmares. Has anything changed recently, or any anniversary dates coming up?

[00:01:56]
Marcus Williams: Yeah, actually. Next week is the anniversary. Four years since the attack. April 10th, 2020. I've been thinking about it more, I guess. And there was this thing at work - a car backfired in the parking lot two days ago and I just... I lost it. Dropped to the ground, heart going crazy. My coworker had to help me up. I was so embarrassed.

[00:02:23]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That must have been frightening and frustrating. Did you explain to your coworker what happened?

[00:02:29]
Marcus Williams: No. I just said I tripped. I don't want people at work knowing about my issues. They already look at me weird when I get jumpy or can't focus. I work in an office now - I'm supposed to be a normal guy in business casual, typing away at spreadsheets. Not some broken soldier who hits the deck when a car backfires.

[00:02:51]
Dr. James Rodriguez: You're not broken, Marcus. What you're experiencing is a normal response to abnormal circumstances. Your nervous system is trying to protect you based on what it learned in a war zone.

[00:03:03]
Marcus Williams: I know you say that, and intellectually I get it. But it doesn't feel normal. It feels like I'm losing my mind. Like I'm stuck in Afghanistan even though I've been home for four years. My body doesn't know the war is over.

[00:03:20]
Marcus Williams: And it's not just the nightmares and the startle response. I can't focus at work. I'll be in a meeting and suddenly I'm gone, you know? I'm back there. I can smell the burning fuel, the dust, the smoke. I can hear the screaming. And then someone asks me a question about quarterly reports and I have no idea what we've been talking about for the last ten minutes.

[00:03:45]
Dr. James Rodriguez: These intrusive memories and flashbacks are really affecting your daily functioning. Are you still avoiding certain places or situations?

[00:03:54]
Marcus Williams: Yeah. I still can't go to the Fourth of July fireworks. Sarah wanted to take the kids last year and I couldn't do it. I sat in the car with noise-canceling headphones while she took them. Felt like a terrible father. My son is five - he keeps asking why Daddy doesn't like fireworks.

[00:04:14]
Marcus Williams: And crowds. I can't do crowds anymore. We were supposed to go to Sarah's company picnic last month and I had a panic attack in the car before we even got there. Too many people, too many variables I can't control. I need to see all the exits, know where everyone is, have my back to a wall. It's exhausting.

[00:04:36]
Dr. James Rodriguez: It sounds like the hypervigilance is really impacting your quality of life. How are you doing with the anger issues we discussed last time?

[00:04:45]
Marcus Williams: Not great. I snapped at Sarah yesterday over something stupid - I think she moved my keys or something. I went from zero to sixty in like two seconds. Started yelling. She just looked at me with this sad expression and walked away. I apologized later but I could see it hurt her. And the worst part is I don't even feel it building. I'm fine one second and then I'm exploding.

[00:05:12]
Marcus Williams: I punched a hole in the garage wall last week. My hand still hurts. I was trying to fix something and it wouldn't work and I just... lost it. My daughter saw. She's eight. She shouldn't have to see her dad like that. She's been quieter around me since then. That kills me.

[00:05:32]
Dr. James Rodriguez: It's good that you're recognizing these patterns, Marcus. That awareness is important. Have you noticed any warning signs before the anger comes?

[00:05:42]
Marcus Williams: Sometimes my jaw gets tight. Or I notice I'm clenching my fists. But like I said, it happens so fast. One minute I'm okay, the next I'm yelling or punching something. I feel like I'm always on the edge, you know? Like I'm waiting for something bad to happen.

[00:06:01]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That's the hypervigilance. Your nervous system is stuck in threat detection mode. You mentioned you can't focus at work - are you sleeping at all, other than when the nightmares wake you up?

[00:06:14]
Marcus Williams: Maybe four hours a night, if I'm lucky. I can't fall asleep until like two or three in the morning. I just lie there, brain going a million miles an hour. Thinking about the guys we lost. Replaying that day over and over. Thinking about different choices I could have made. If I'd just insisted we take a different route, maybe Rodriguez and Davis would still be alive.

[00:06:39]
Dr. James Rodriguez: We've talked about survivor's guilt before. Are those thoughts getting worse as we approach the anniversary?

[00:06:46]
Marcus Williams: Yeah. Way worse. I keep thinking, why did I make it out and they didn't? Rodriguez had a wife and a baby. Davis was getting married in two months. I see their faces every time I close my eyes. I should have been able to save them. I was the driver - it was my job to keep everyone safe.

[00:07:08]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Marcus, we've been over this. You didn't plant that IED. You didn't start that war. You were doing your job under incredibly difficult circumstances. The attack wasn't your fault.

[00:07:21]
Marcus Williams: I know that's what I'm supposed to believe. But it doesn't change the fact that they're dead and I'm here. And for what? So I can yell at my wife and scare my kids and freak out over a car backfiring? Some life I'm giving them.

[00:07:38]
Marcus Williams: Sometimes I think they'd be better off without me. I'm not suicidal or anything - I would never do that to them. But I think about how much easier their lives would be if I wasn't bringing all this baggage into it. Sarah could marry someone normal. Someone who doesn't wake up screaming. Someone who can take their kids to fireworks and doesn't lose their temper over stupid stuff.

[00:08:03]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Marcus, I need to ask you directly - are you having thoughts of harming yourself?

[00:08:09]
Marcus Williams: No. I mean, I think about not existing sometimes, but I would never act on it. I've seen what suicide does to families. One of my buddies from the unit ate his gun three years ago and it destroyed his whole family. I wouldn't do that. I have my kids to think about.

[00:08:28]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Okay. I appreciate you being honest with me about that. But I want you to promise me that if those thoughts ever get more serious, or if you start thinking about a plan, you'll call me or go to the ER. Can you promise me that?

[00:08:42]
Marcus Williams: Yeah, I promise. Like I said, I'm not there. I'm just... tired. Tired of fighting my own brain every day. Tired of being afraid. Tired of feeling guilty for being alive.

[00:08:56]
Dr. James Rodriguez: I hear you, Marcus. And I want you to know that what you're experiencing is treatable. PTSD doesn't have to be a life sentence. But we need to be more aggressive with treatment. I think it's time we seriously considered medication alongside the therapy.

[00:09:13]
Marcus Williams: I don't know, Doc. I just... what if it changes who I am? What if I become a zombie? What if I can't feel anything?

[00:09:23]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Those are valid concerns, and they're also misconceptions about modern PTSD medications. We're not talking about turning you into a zombie. We're talking about medication that can help regulate your nervous system so you can actually engage with the therapy we're doing. Right now, your brain is so flooded with stress hormones that it's hard for you to use the skills we're practicing.

[00:09:47]
Marcus Williams: What kind of medication are we talking about?

[00:09:51]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Typically, we'd start with an SSRI - Sertraline or Paroxetine are FDA-approved for PTSD. They can help with the depression, anxiety, and intrusive thoughts. We'd start at a low dose and see how you respond. Some people also benefit from Prazosin specifically for nightmares.

[00:10:10]
Marcus Williams: And what are the side effects?

[00:10:14]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Common ones are nausea, headache, maybe some sleep changes initially. Sexual side effects are possible. But everyone responds differently, and we can adjust if you're having issues. The goal is to find something that gives you relief without intolerable side effects.

[00:10:31]
Marcus Williams: Can I think about it? Talk to Sarah?

[00:10:35]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Of course. This is your decision. But I want you to consider that what you're doing now isn't working. The nightmares are getting worse, the flashbacks are interfering with work, you're isolated from activities you used to enjoy, and your relationships are suffering. Medication could be the tool that helps you get to a place where you can actually heal.

[00:10:56]
Marcus Williams: You're right. I know you're right. I'm just scared, you know? Feels like admitting I can't handle it on my own.

[00:11:05]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Taking medication isn't a sign of weakness, Marcus. You wouldn't refuse insulin if you were diabetic. Your brain chemistry has been altered by trauma. This is a medical issue, not a character flaw.

[00:11:18]
Marcus Williams: Okay. Yeah. Let me talk to Sarah and I'll let you know by next session. She'll probably be relieved, honestly. She's been worried about me.

[00:11:28]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That sounds like a good plan. Now, let's talk about strategies for getting through this anniversary week. What's your plan for April 10th?

[00:11:37]
Marcus Williams: I don't know. I took the day off work. I didn't want to have a breakdown at the office. But then I don't know what to do with myself. Sitting at home alone with my thoughts doesn't seem great either.

[00:11:51]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Have you thought about creating a memorial ritual? Something to honor Rodriguez and Davis? Some people find it helpful to have a structured way to remember rather than trying to avoid the day entirely.

[00:12:05]
Marcus Williams: Like what?

[00:12:07]
Dr. James Rodriguez: It could be anything meaningful to you. Some veterans visit the memorial wall. Some write letters to the people they lost. Some do an act of service in their honor. What feels right to you?

[00:12:20]
Marcus Williams: I don't know. Maybe... Rodriguez loved baseball. He was always talking about the Dodgers. Maybe I could take my son to a game, you know? Do something Rodriguez would have wanted to do with his kid.

[00:12:36]
Dr. James Rodriguez: I think that's a beautiful idea. It honors Rodriguez's memory while also connecting with your son. How do you feel about that plan?

[00:12:44]
Marcus Williams: It feels good, actually. Like I'm doing something positive instead of just sitting in my guilt all day. And maybe I can tell my son about Rodriguez. Keep his memory alive.

[00:12:58]
Dr. James Rodriguez: I think that's a healthy way to process the anniversary. And Marcus, if it gets too overwhelming, I want you to reach out. You have my emergency number. You also have the Veterans Crisis Line number, right?

[00:13:12]
Marcus Williams: Yeah, I have it. Thanks, Doc. I'll use it if I need to. Though honestly, talking to Sarah helps. When I can actually tell her what's going on instead of just shutting down.

[00:13:24]
Dr. James Rodriguez: How is your communication with Sarah overall?

[00:13:29]
Marcus Williams: Better than it was. We're actually talking about this stuff now instead of me just pretending everything is fine. But I know it's hard on her. She didn't sign up to be a caretaker. She married a soldier, not a basket case.

[00:13:45]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Sarah married you, Marcus. All of you, including the parts that are struggling. Has she said she wants to leave or that this is too much?

[00:13:54]
Marcus Williams: No. She keeps saying she's in it for the long haul. She went to that support group for spouses of veterans a few times. I think that helped her understand that she's not alone in dealing with this.

[00:14:07]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That's great that she's getting support too. This affects the whole family system. How are your kids doing?

[00:14:14]
Marcus Williams: I worry about them. My daughter asked me the other day why I'm so sad all the time. I didn't even realize I was - I thought I was hiding it. But kids pick up on everything, you know? I don't want them growing up thinking Dad is always angry or sad or scared.

[00:14:32]
Marcus Williams: And my son - he's at that age where he thinks military stuff is cool. He plays with toy soldiers and asks me about my time in the Army. I don't know how to talk to him about it. How do you explain war to a five-year-old? How do you tell him that it's not actually cool, that people die and it stays with you forever?

[00:14:53]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That's a really difficult balance. What have you been telling him so far?

[00:14:59]
Marcus Williams: Just basic stuff. That I helped people, that I worked with a team, that it was my job. I don't tell him about the combat or the deaths. I don't want to scare him or give him nightmares too.

[00:15:13]
Dr. James Rodriguez: I think that's age-appropriate. You don't need to share details that would be traumatizing for him. But it might help to have simple, honest answers prepared for when he asks questions. Something like "Some parts of being a soldier were difficult, and I'm still working through those."

[00:15:30]
Marcus Williams: Yeah, that makes sense. I just don't want to lie to him, but I also don't want to burden him with stuff he can't understand.

[00:15:39]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Exactly. You're finding that balance. Now, let's talk about your avoidance behaviors. You mentioned you can't do fireworks or crowds. What else are you avoiding?

[00:15:51]
Marcus Williams: I can't watch war movies anymore. Even the news - if there's coverage of anything in the Middle East, I have to change the channel. I stopped going to the gym because it's too crowded. I don't go to bars or restaurants during peak hours. I basically structure my whole life around avoiding triggers.

[00:16:12]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That's a lot of avoidance. And while it might feel safer in the short term, it's actually reinforcing the PTSD. Every time you avoid something, you're telling your brain that the threat is real.

[00:16:25]
Marcus Williams: So what am I supposed to do? Just force myself into situations that make me panic?

[00:16:30]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Not force yourself, but gradually, systematically expose yourself to triggers in a controlled way. That's what exposure therapy is for. We've touched on this before, but I think it's time to really commit to it.

[00:16:44]
Marcus Williams: I don't know if I can do that, Doc. Just thinking about going to a crowded place makes my heart race.

[00:16:51]
Dr. James Rodriguez: We'd start very small. Maybe just sitting in a parking lot of a busy store, not even going in. Building up your tolerance slowly. And we'd do it together, in a controlled way, with tools to manage your anxiety.

[00:17:06]
Marcus Williams: Okay. I guess I can try. It's not like what I'm doing now is working.

[00:17:13]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That's the spirit. We'll create a hierarchy - a list of situations from least to most anxiety-provoking. And we'll tackle them one at a time, at your pace. The goal isn't to make you comfortable with everything immediately. The goal is to show your brain that you can handle these situations and survive them.

[00:17:33]
Marcus Williams: Alright. I'm willing to try. But if I freak out, you're dealing with it.

[00:17:39]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Deal. That's what I'm here for. Now, let's check in on the drinking. Last time you said you were trying to cut back. How's that going?

[00:17:50]
Marcus Williams: Better, actually. I was up to like six or seven beers a night, and I've got it down to two or three. Sarah noticed. She said she's proud of me. But I'll be honest, it's hard. That's how I used to shut my brain off at night, you know? Now I'm just lying there awake with nothing to dull the thoughts.

[00:18:12]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That's actually really good progress, Marcus. Cutting it in half is significant. The sleep issues are tough, though. Have you been using the sleep hygiene strategies we talked about?

[00:18:24]
Marcus Williams: Sort of. I try to keep the bedroom cool and dark. I don't look at my phone before bed anymore. But my mind still races. I'll do the breathing exercises and they help a little, but then I just lie there thinking about everything.

[00:18:40]
Dr. James Rodriguez: The medication we discussed could help with that too. SSRIs can help with sleep, and Prazosin specifically reduces nightmares. You might actually be able to get a full night's sleep.

[00:18:52]
Marcus Williams: Man, I can't even remember what that feels like. I think the last time I slept through the night was before I deployed in 2018. Six years of terrible sleep. No wonder I'm a mess.

[00:19:06]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Sleep deprivation compounds everything else. It makes the flashbacks worse, the irritability worse, the concentration issues worse. Getting your sleep under control could be a game-changer for you.

[00:19:20]
Marcus Williams: Okay, you've convinced me. I'll talk to Sarah tonight about the medication. I think I'm ready to try it.

[00:19:28]
Dr. James Rodriguez: I'm glad to hear that, Marcus. I think this could really help. We'll start low and go slow, and I'll be checking in with you frequently to see how you're doing.

[00:19:39]
Marcus Williams: Thanks, Doc. I appreciate you not giving up on me. I know I'm not the easiest patient.

[00:19:46]
Dr. James Rodriguez: You're doing the work, Marcus. That's what matters. Recovery isn't linear - there are going to be tough weeks, especially around anniversaries. But you keep showing up, you keep trying, and that takes courage.

[00:20:00]
Marcus Williams: Doesn't feel like courage. Feels like survival.

[00:20:04]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Sometimes they're the same thing. Now, before we wrap up, I want to make sure you have a safety plan for this week. What are your warning signs that you need extra support?

[00:20:18]
Marcus Williams: If I start drinking more again. If I can't get out of bed. If I start having thoughts about hurting myself. If I feel like I'm going to hurt someone else out of anger.

[00:20:31]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Good. And what will you do if you notice those signs?

[00:20:36]
Marcus Williams: Call you. Go to the ER if it's really bad. Call the Veterans Crisis Line. Tell Sarah what's going on.

[00:20:45]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Perfect. And remember, you can call me between sessions if you need to check in. Don't wait until you're in crisis.

[00:20:54]
Marcus Williams: I will. Thank you.

[00:20:57]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Before you go, let's set our homework for this week. I want you to do two things. First, practice the grounding technique at least once a day, not just when you're panicking. The more you practice it when you're calm, the easier it will be to use when you need it.

[00:21:16]
Marcus Williams: Okay, I can do that.

[00:21:18]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Second, I want you to start a journal specifically about the anniversary. Write down memories of Rodriguez and Davis - the good ones, not just the day they died. Write about what you learned from them, what you valued about them. We're going to use that for your memorial ritual.

[00:21:37]
Marcus Williams: That's going to be hard. But I'll try.

[00:21:41]
Dr. James Rodriguez: I know it will be. But honoring their lives, not just their deaths, is an important part of processing this. And finally, have that conversation with Sarah about medication and let me know what you decide.

[00:21:55]
Marcus Williams: Got it. Grounding practice, journal about Rodriguez and Davis, talk to Sarah about meds. I can do that.

[00:22:03]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Great. We'll check in on all of this next week. How are you feeling right now, before we end?

[00:22:11]
Marcus Williams: Honestly? A little lighter. Like there's actually a plan instead of just drowning. I'm still scared about the medication and the exposure therapy stuff, but at least I feel like we're doing something, you know?

[00:22:26]
Dr. James Rodriguez: That's exactly where we want to be - taking action instead of just surviving. You're doing good work here, Marcus. I know it doesn't always feel like it, but you're making progress.

[00:22:38]
Marcus Williams: Thanks, Doc. I'll see you next week. Same time?

[00:22:42]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Same time. Take care of yourself, Marcus. And remember - reach out if you need to.

[00:22:48]
Marcus Williams: I will. Thanks again.

[00:22:51]
Dr. James Rodriguez: You're welcome. Have a good week.

[00:22:54]
Marcus Williams: You too.

[Session ends 00:22:57]

# Audio Transcription & Speaker Diarization — Design Document

_Created: 2026-03-03_
_Status: DRAFT — awaiting review_

---

## 1. Problem Statement

Pablo Companion records therapy sessions (audio) on the therapist's laptop. Today, those recordings are uploaded as raw audio to the Pablo backend. The backend (and downstream SOAP note generation) needs **text transcripts with speaker labels** in a structured format similar to Google Meet transcripts.

### Requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
|-------------|--------|
| **Transcription** | Speech-to-text from recorded WAV files |
| **Speaker diarization** | "Who said what" — label each segment by speaker |
| **Speaker identification** | Map diarized clusters to known roles: THERAPIST, CLIENT_A, CLIENT_B |
| **Session types** | 1:1 (therapist + patient) and couples counseling (therapist + 2 clients) |
| **HIPAA compliance** | PHI must be protected at rest, in transit, and during processing |
| **Processing mode** | Batch (after session ends) — not real-time |
| **Compute location** | Local-only (default) with optional cloud mode for quality/speed boost |
| **Output format** | Structured transcript matching Google Meet format (the downstream session pipeline expects this) |
| **Stereo playback** | System audio may be stereo; preserve stereo for human playback while processing mono for ASR |

### Non-Requirements (for this design)

- Real-time / live transcription during session
- Language translation
- Medical terminology custom dictionary (future enhancement)

Yes exactly — the user's message is exactly the right insight: "we could ask AudioCaptureKit to create the best quality file it can in separate files." This simplifies the API design:

- Don't ask AudioCaptureKit to do resampling or channel manipulation
- Just ask it to output separate files at its best quality (48kHz, 16-bit, mono for mic, stereo for system)
- The Rust core handles resampling and any other preprocessing for ASR

Now I have everything I need. Let me write both design documents:

1. **Update `docs/audio-transcription-design.md`**: Fix the transcript format section (exact format from sample), update Q2 and Q3 (system settings not org), update the recording section to say "best quality separate files from AudioCaptureKit"


2. **Create `docs/audiocapturekit-separate-streams-design.md`**: Design doc for another Claude Code to implement the AudioCaptureKit API changes. I need to document the transcript format structure with timestamps, speaker names, and session metadata, then outline how AudioCaptureKit will capture and output the separate audio streams.

For the separate streams approach, the user wants maximum quality without mixing: the microphone stream should be mono at 48kHz 16-bit, the system audio should be stereo at 48kHz 16-bit, and AudioCaptureKit just needs to write them to separate files while the Rust core handles any resampling for ASR downstream.

Now I'm examining the current `CaptureConfiguration` structure to understand what parameters need to be exposed in the API design... Rate: 48000,
    bitDepth: 16,
    channels: 2,
    encryptor: encryptor,
    outputDirectory: recordingsDirectory,
    micDeviceID: selectedMicID,
    enableMicCapture: debugEnableMic,
    enableSystemCapture: debugEnableSystem
)
```

And `RecordingResult` has: `fileURL`, `duration`, `metadata` (with `.id`, `.isEncrypted`), `checksum`

The API changes needed:
1. New: `AudioStreamMode` enum in `CaptureConfiguration`
   - `.combined` — current behavior (default for backward compat)
   - `.separateFiles` — new behavior
2. When `.separateFiles`:
   - Mic: mono, 48kHz, 16-bit
   - System: stereo (2-channel), 48kHz, 16-bit
3. `RecordingResult` gains optional `systemAudioFileURL: URL?`
   - In `.combined` mode: nil
   - In `.separateFiles` mode: URL to system audio file
   - `fileURL` points to mic file in `.separateFiles` mode (or the combined file in `.combined` mode)
4. File naming: existing convention + `_mic` / `_system` suffixes in `.separateFiles` mode
5. Encryption: applies to both files when enabled
6. For HIPAA compliance, each file needs its own checksum for integrity verification, so I'm thinking about whether to store separate checksums in the result struct...

Defining the RecordingResult structure...

Now I need to extend `LocalRecording` in pablo-companion to handle both URLs, then write up the design docs comprehensively.

content.text: Yes exactly right — that's the cleanest model. AudioCaptureKit does what it does best (capture high-quality audio), you just ask it to keep the streams separate. No resampling, no DSP, no mixing. The Rust core handles all of that downstream when it needs 16kHz mono for ASR. Let me write both docs now.

# AudioCaptureKit: Separate Audio Streams — Design Document

_Created: 2026-03-03_
_Status: READY FOR IMPLEMENTATION_
_Target repo: pablo-health/AudioCaptureKit_

---

## 1. Context

AudioCaptureKit is a first-party Pablo framework used in Pablo Companion (and potentially other products) to record therapy sessions on macOS. Currently it captures mic + system audio and writes both streams into a single mixed WAV file.

The Pablo transcription pipeline needs these streams **separated** so that:
- The mic file (therapist's voice) can be transcribed with a known speaker identity
- The system audio file (remote participant voices) can be transcribed and diarized independently
- Stereo system audio is preserved for high-quality human playback

AudioCaptureKit's job is simply to **capture at the best quality it can and write separate files**. It does not resample, mix, or do any ML processing. Downstream consumers (Rust core, ASR pipeline) handle resampling and channel manipulation.

---

## 2. Current API (as observed in pablo-companion)

```swift
// CaptureConfiguration
let config = CaptureConfiguration(
    sampleRate: 48000,
    bitDepth: 16,
    channels: 2,           // currently mixes mic + system into 2-ch file
    encryptor: encryptor,
    outputDirectory: recordingsDirectory,
    micDeviceID: selectedMicID,
    enableMicCapture: true,
    enableSystemCapture: true
)

// RecordingResult (returned by stopCapture())
result.fileURL        // single WAV file (mic + system mixed)
result.duration       // TimeInterval
result.metadata.id    // UUID
result.metadata.isEncrypted // Bool
result.checksum       // String (integrity check)
```

---

## 3. Requirements

### 3.1 New Behavior: Separate Output Files

When configured for separate output, AudioCaptureKit must write **two files**:

| File | Channels | Content | Format |
|------|----------|---------|--------|
| Mic file | **1 (mono)** | Microphone input only — no system audio | 48 kHz, 16-bit PCM WAV |
| System audio file | **2 (stereo)** | System/loopback audio only — no mic | 48 kHz, 16-bit PCM WAV |

**Key principle:** Capture at full quality, do not downsample. Consumers decide what to do with the audio.

### 3.2 File Naming

Use the same base naming convention as the existing single-file mode, with suffixes:

```
<existing_base_name>_mic.wav       // microphone
<existing_base_name>_system.wav    // system audio
```

Example: if the existing convention produces `recording-2026-03-03T14-30-00.wav`, produce:
```
recording-2026-03-03T14-30-00_mic.wav
recording-2026-03-03T14-30-00_system.wav
```

### 3.3 Encryption

If an encryptor is provided, **both files** are encrypted independently. Each file gets its own encrypted output.

### 3.4 Checksums

Each file gets its own checksum for integrity verification (HIPAA compliance requires this).

### 3.5 Backward Compatibility

The default behavior is **unchanged**. Existing callers passing no `streamMode` get the current single-file combined output. No existing code in pablo-companion or other consumers should break.

### 3.6 Partial Capture

Handle gracefully:
- If only mic capture is enabled → produce only mic file
- If only system capture is enabled → produce only system file
- If neither system audio is available (user hasn't granted permission) → proceed with mic-only, no system file

---

## 4. Proposed API Changes

### 4.1 New Enum: `AudioStreamMode`

```swift
/// Controls whether AudioCaptureKit mixes audio streams into one file
/// or writes each source to a separate file.
public enum AudioStreamMode: Sendable {
    /// Mic and system audio are mixed into a single stereo WAV file.
    /// This is the existing behavior and the default.
    case combined

    /// Mic and system audio are written as separate files.
    /// Mic: mono WAV.  System audio: stereo WAV.
    /// fileURL in RecordingResult points to the mic file.
    /// systemAudioFileURL in RecordingResult points to the system audio file.
    case separateFiles
}
```

### 4.2 Updated `CaptureConfiguration`

Add one new property. All existing properties and their defaults are unchanged.

```swift
public struct CaptureConfiguration: Sendable {
    // --- EXISTING PROPERTIES (unchanged) ---
    public var sampleRate: Int
    public var bitDepth: Int
    public var channels: Int
    public var encryptor: (any RecordingEncryptor)?
    public var outputDirectory: URL
    public var micDeviceID: String?
    public var enableMicCapture: Bool
    public var enableSystemCapture: Bool

    // --- NEW PROPERTY ---
    /// Controls whether mic and system audio are written to a single file
    /// (combined, existing behavior) or two separate files.
    /// Default: .combined (preserves backward compatibility)
    public var streamMode: AudioStreamMode = .combined

    // Existing init remains unchanged — new property uses default value
}
```

### 4.3 Updated `RecordingResult`

Add one new optional property. All existing properties are unchanged.

```swift
public struct RecordingResult: Sendable {
    // --- EXISTING PROPERTIES (unchanged) ---
    /// Primary output file.
    /// - In .combined mode: the single mixed WAV file (existing behavior).
    /// - In .separateFiles mode: the microphone-only WAV file.
    public let fileURL: URL

    public let duration: TimeInterval
    public let metadata: RecordingMetadata
    public let checksum: String  // checksum of fileURL

    // --- NEW PROPERTIES ---
    /// System audio output file. Only present when streamMode == .separateFiles
    /// AND system audio was successfully captured.
    /// nil in .combined mode or if no system audio was captured.
    public let systemAudioFileURL: URL?

    /// Checksum of systemAudioFileURL. nil when systemAudioFileURL is nil.
    public let systemAudioChecksum: String?
}
```

### 4.4 No Other API Changes

- `CompositeCaptureSession` public interface is unchanged
- `AudioCaptureDelegate` protocol is unchanged
- `CaptureState`, `AudioLevels`, `CaptureError`, `AudioSource` are unchanged
- All existing initializers continue to work

---

## 5. Internal Implementation Guidance

> Note: This section is for the implementer. The public API is what matters; implementation details may vary.

### 5.1 Recording Architecture

In `.combined` mode (existing): a single `AVAudioFile` or similar writer receives mixed audio.

In `.separateFiles` mode (new):
- Maintain two separate audio writers: one for mic, one for system audio
- Write mic samples only to the mic writer
- Write system audio samples only to the system audio writer
- Do not cross-contaminate streams

### 5.2 Channel Configuration

In `.separateFiles` mode, the `channels` parameter in `CaptureConfiguration` applies to the **combined** mode only (for backward compat). In separate mode:
- Mic file: always 1 channel (mono)
- System file: always 2 channels (stereo)

The implementer may choose to ignore the `channels` value in `.separateFiles` mode or derive the channel count from the capture source.

### 5.3 Silence / No Capture

In `.separateFiles` mode:
- If `enableMicCapture = false` → no mic file produced → `fileURL` should still be valid but contains silence or an empty duration, OR return a meaningful error. **Preferred:** if mic is the primary file, treat a disabled mic as an error in `.separateFiles` mode.
- If `enableSystemCapture = false` OR system audio is unavailable → `systemAudioFileURL = nil`, `systemAudioChecksum = nil`. This is not an error.

### 5.4 Timing Alignment

Both files must share the same start timestamp and session ID so that consumers can align them. The `metadata.id` (UUID) must be identical in both files' metadata.

### 5.5 Encryption

If an encryptor is provided:
1. Write the raw WAV to a temp file
2. Encrypt the temp file → final output file
3. Delete the temp file
4. Repeat for each file (mic, system) independently

### 5.6 Checksum

Compute checksum over the final output file bytes (after encryption if encrypted). Use the same checksum algorithm as the existing implementation.

---

## 6. How pablo-companion Uses This

After this change, `RecordingService.swift` in pablo-companion will be updated to:

```swift
let config = CaptureConfiguration(
    sampleRate: 48000,
    bitDepth: 16,
    channels: 2,         // only relevant for .combined (ignored in .separateFiles)
    encryptor: encryptor,
    outputDirectory: recordingsDirectory,
    micDeviceID: selectedMicID,
    enableMicCapture: true,
    enableSystemCapture: true,
    streamMode: .separateFiles   // NEW
)
```

And `RecordingService` will receive:
```swift
let result = try await session.stopCapture()
result.fileURL              // mic file (therapist)
result.systemAudioFileURL   // system audio file (client(s)), may be nil
result.checksum             // mic file checksum
result.systemAudioChecksum  // system audio checksum, may be nil
```

The pablo-companion `LocalRecording` model will be updated to store both URLs. The AudioCaptureKit design doc does not need to specify those downstream changes.

---

## 7. Testing Requirements

### 7.1 Unit Tests

- `.combined` mode produces a single file (existing behavior unchanged)
- `.separateFiles` mode with both sources enabled: produces two files, mic is mono, system is stereo
- `.separateFiles` mode with system audio unavailable: produces mic file only, `systemAudioFileURL` is nil
- `.separateFiles` mode with encryption: both files are encrypted, checksums are valid
- File naming: mic file ends with `_mic.wav`, system file ends with `_system.wav`
- Both files share the same `metadata.id`
- Duration of both files matches the session duration

### 7.2 Manual / Integration Tests

- Record a real session (requires mic + screen recording permission)
- Verify mic file contains only mic audio (no echo of system audio)
- Verify system file contains only system audio (no mic bleed)
- Verify both files play back correctly in QuickTime / AVFoundation
- Verify checksum validation passes for both files
- Verify encrypted files decrypt correctly

---

## 8. Definition of Done

- [ ] `AudioStreamMode` enum is public and documented
- [ ] `CaptureConfiguration.streamMode` added with default `.combined`
- [ ] `RecordingResult.systemAudioFileURL` and `systemAudioChecksum` added as optionals
- [ ] `.combined` mode behavior is byte-for-byte identical to the existing implementation (no regression)
- [ ] `.separateFiles` mode produces correct mono mic file and stereo system file
- [ ] Encryption works for both files in `.separateFiles` mode
- [ ] File naming follows `_mic` / `_system` suffix convention
- [ ] Both files share the same `metadata.id`
- [ ] All unit tests pass
- [ ] `make check` (or equivalent) passes with zero warnings

# AudioCaptureKit: Separate Audio Streams — Design Document

_Created: 2026-03-03_
_Status: READY FOR IMPLEMENTATION_
_Target repo: pablo-health/AudioCaptureKit_

---

## 1. Context

AudioCaptureKit is a first-party Pablo framework used in Pablo Companion (and potentially other products) to record therapy sessions on macOS. Currently it captures mic + system audio and writes both streams into a single mixed WAV file.

The Pablo transcription pipeline needs these streams **separated** so that:
- The mic file (therapist's voice) can be transcribed with a known speaker identity
- The system audio file (remote participant voices) can be transcribed and diarized independently
- Stereo system audio is preserved for high-quality human playback

AudioCaptureKit's job is simply to **capture at the best quality it can and write separate files**. It does not resample, mix, or do any ML processing. Downstream consumers (Rust core, ASR pipeline) handle resampling and channel manipulation.

---

## 2. Current API (as observed in pablo-companion)

```swift

    sampleRate: 48000,
    bitDepth: 16,
    channels: 2,           // currently mixes mic + system into 2-ch file
    encryptor: encryptor,
    outputDirectory: recordingsDirectory,
    micDeviceID: selectedMicID,
    enableMicCapture: true,
    enableSystemCapture: true
)

// RecordingResult (returned by stopCapture())
result.fileURL        // single WAV file (mic + system mixed)
result.duration       // TimeInterval
result.metadata.id    // UUID
result.metadata.isEncrypted // Bool
result.checksum       // String (integrity check)
```

## 3. Requirements

### 3.1 New Behavior: Separate Output Files

When configured for separate output, AudioCaptureKit must write **two files**:

| File | Channels | Content | Format |
|------|----------|---------|--------|
| Mic file | **1 (mono)** | Microphone input only — no system audio | 48 kHz, 16-bit PCM WAV |
| System audio file | **2 (stereo)** | System/loopback audio only — no mic | 48 kHz, 16-bit PCM WAV |

**Key principle:** Capture at full quality, do not downsample. Consumers decide what to do with the audio.

### 3.2 File Naming

Use the same base naming convention as the existing single-file mode, with suffixes:

```
<existing_base_name>_mic.wav       // microphone
<existing_base_name>_system.wav    // system audio
```

Example: if the existing convention produces `recording-2026-03-03T14-30-00.wav`, produce:

recording-2026-03-03T14-30-00_system.wav

    /// systemAudioFileURL in RecordingResult points to the system audio file.
    case separateFiles
}
```

### 4.2 Updated `CaptureConfiguration`

Add one new property. All existing properties and their defaults are unchanged.

```swift
public struct CaptureConfiguration: Sendable {
    // --- EXISTING PROPERTIES (unchanged) ---
    public var sampleRate: Int
    public var bitDepth: Int

3. Delete the temp file

### Google Meet Format Export

The downstream session pipeline expects a Google Meet–style transcript. Render from canonical JSON:

```
00:00:00 Dr. Lee
Good afternoon. How has the week been since we last met?

00:00:04 Alex
It's been rough. I couldn't sleep much this week.

00:00:12 Jordan
I noticed that too. There's been a lot of tension at home.

00:00:23 Dr. Lee
Can you both tell me more about what's been creating that tension?
```

**Rendering rules:**
- Timestamp: `HH:MM:SS` at turn-level (start of first segment in the turn)
- Speaker: `display_name` from speaker map
- Merge adjacent segments from same speaker if gap ≤ 1.5 seconds
- Start new turn if: speaker changes, OR pause > 3 seconds (even if same speaker)
- Overlap: tag `[overlapping]` inline if `overlap: true` (optional, depends on downstream needs)

| Setting | Options | Default | Notes |
|---------|---------|---------|-------|
| **Processing mode** | Local / Cloud | Local | Org-level override possible |
| **Quality preset** | Fast / Balanced / High Accuracy | Balanced | Maps to Whisper model size |
| **Session type** | 1:1 / Couples | 1:1 | Affects diarization (2 vs 3 speakers) |
| **Auto-transcribe** | On / Off | On | Start transcription automatically when recording stops |

input.new_string:
### Google Meet Format Export

The downstream session pipeline expects a Google Meet–style transcript. This format has been confirmed from real session data (see `meeting-transcription` repo synthetic samples).

**Exact format:**

```
Google Meet Transcript
Session Date: April 3, 2024
Duration: 52:14

[00:00:08]
Dr. James Rodriguez: Hey Marcus, good to see you. How have you been since our last session?

[00:00:13]
Marcus Williams: Hey Doc. I've been... it's been a rough week, honestly.

[00:00:18]
Dr. James Rodriguez: I'm sorry to hear that. Do you want to tell me what's been going on?

[Session ends 00:22:57]

---
Total Duration: 52:14
Speakers: 2
Dr. James Rodriguez (Therapist)
Marcus Williams (Client)
```

**Format specification:**
- Line 1: literal `Google Meet Transcript`
- Line 2: `Session Date: <Month D, YYYY>`
- Line 3: `Duration: <MM:SS or H:MM:SS>` (total session duration)
- Line 4: blank
- For each turn:
  - `[HH:MM:SS]` — timestamp on its own line (format: always 2-digit hours)
  - `Speaker Display Name: utterance text` — on the next line
  - Blank line after each turn
- Session end marker: `[Session ends HH:MM:SS]`
- Blank line
- `---` separator
- `Total Duration: <same as header Duration>`
- `Speakers: N`
- One line per speaker: `Display Name (Role)` — roles are: `Therapist`, `Client`, `Client - <description if relevant>`
- Blank line (no `Clinical Notes` section — that is added downstream by the SOAP note pipeline, not by us)

**Rendering rules from canonical JSON:**
- Timestamp: use `start` time of first segment in the turn, formatted as `[HH:MM:SS]`
- Speaker name: `display_name` from speaker map
- Merge adjacent segments from same speaker if gap ≤ 1.5 seconds → single turn
- Start new turn if: speaker changes, OR pause > 3 seconds within same speaker
- No inline overlap tags — if overlap detected, attribute to dominant speaker
- `Session Date` comes from session metadata (session scheduled date, not file creation date)
- `Duration` comes from canonical JSON `duration_seconds`
